Title: Motorcyclist lies motionless after being hit by car on SUKE highway
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A rider is seen lying motionless on the road after a blue car rammed into him on the Sungai Besi–Ulu Kelang Elevated Expressway (SUKE) in a video that has gone viral on social media.
The 1 minute 18 second clip, believed to be rear-facing dashcam footage from a passing vehicle, has been widely circulated on Facebook and WhatsApp.
It shows the motorcyclist riding on the right side of the expressway while overtaking a red car.
Another car then speeds past the red car before crashing into the motorcyclist.
The rider is flung onto the car's bonnet before being thrown onto the road.
He is seen lying in the middle of the road as the blue car speeds off.
Police have been contacted for comment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King, Queen grace Aidilfitri open house at Johor's Istana Besar
Article:
JOHOR BARU: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, graced the Aidilfitri open house at Istana Besar here today.
The King was accompanied by Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia; the Regent of Johor Tunku Mahkota Ismail; his consort Che' Puan Mahkota Khaleeda Johor; and, Their Royal Highnesses' children.
Also present were Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi and his wife Datin Sharmin Fazlina Mohd Shukor.
More than 20,000 people of various races attended the Istana Johor open house, as members of the public began queuing outside the palace gates as early as 6.30am.
Many arrived with their families to experience the festive celebration at the palace, which featured more than 17 stalls offering a variety of traditional and modern dishes.
Among the crowd favourites were ketupat, lemang, rendang and KFC, as well as various traditional kuih and many other delicacies.
The Queen then spent time greeting members of the public in attendance, accompanied by Tunku Mahkota Ismail and Che' Puan Mahkota Khaleeda.
Guests told Bernama that they were excited to have the opportunity to meet members of the royal family.
Civil servant Zairi Karim, 42, said that although this was his fifth time attending the palace open house, he and his family would continue to come even if they had to queue for a long time.
"I will still come because I want to meet our beloved royal family. I am from Johor, I love my Sultan. I started queuing as early as 8.30am, and only managed to enter an hour later," said the father of four.
Sharing the same sentiment, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia (UTM) student from Nigeria, Adamu Chiroma, who was dressed in full baju Melayu, said he was happy to celebrate the festive occasion in a foreign country.
"You know, when you are in a country, or when you meet people, you need to adapt and become a part of it. That's what makes me wear a baju Melayu and feel joyful. Today, the food is very good, and Malaysians are very generous," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thai authorities halt sand dredging activity on Malaysian side of Golok River
Article:
KOTA BARU: Thai authorities have halted sand dredging activities along the Malaysian side of Golok River following complaints raised through bilateral border coordination channels.
Sungai Golok district operations centre director Supeeyan Daemokleng said the action was taken on March 17 after the centre received a report on the activity from Narathiwat authorities.
He said officials in Narathiwat were alerted by their Malaysian counterparts via the Thai-Malaysian border coordination office under the military.
"The complaint alleged that an excavator had been deployed in the Golok River to dredge sand along the Malaysian riverbank.
"The activity sparked concern among Malaysian authorities.
"Based on our initial investigations, the activity was part of riverbank protection works carried out by a Thai contractor," he said.
Supeeyan, who is also the Sungai Golok district officer, said he deployed a team to the site on March 17 to verify the complaint.
"The team met with the company involved and issued a stern warning prohibiting the use of excavators to dredge sand from the river.
"Strict legal action will be taken against any party found violating the directive," he said, adding that the move was aimed at addressing Malaysia's concerns and ensuring compliance with regulations along the shared waterway.
Meanwhile, residents in Rantau Panjang have expressed concern that the sand dredging activities could cause the Golok River to become shallower, potentially affecting its flow.
Mohamad Naziz Abdul Zaman said he hoped both Malaysian and Thai authorities would take firm and coordinated action to prevent such activities from recurring.
"Any changes to the river's structure could have long-term environmental effects," he said.
He added that continuous monitoring and strict enforcement were crucial to protecting the river.
The Golok River is a narrow river forming part of the border between Malaysia and Thailand in Kelantan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CPO futures seen trading higher next week amid rising energy prices
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Crude palm oil (CPO) futures on Bursa Malaysia Derivatives are expected to trade with an upward bias next week amid the conflict in West Asia.
Iceberg X Sdn Bhd proprietary trader David Ng said the conflict has pushed energy prices higher, lending support to sentiment on palm oil.
"The ongoing war involving Iran, the United States and Israel is driving oil prices higher, which is supporting palm oil sentiment in the market.
"We see prices ranging between RM4,450 and RM4,680 (per tonne) next week," he told Bernama.
In contrast, Interband Group of Companies senior palm oil trader Jim Teh said the market is expected to turn bearish next week amid a shortened trading period due to Aidilfitri celebrations.
Teh said the market will likely see profit-taking activities, with prices moving in the RM4,100-RM4,200 level due to higher energy prices and logistics disruption in West Asia amid the ongoing conflict, while speculators may enter the market to take advantage of price movements.
"There will be some logistical disruption to West Asia because of the war that is going on to make the speculators come in and speculate on the prices," he said.
On stock levels, he said Malaysia currently has ample palm oil inventories, while demand is still expected from key importers such as China, Pakistan, India, the European Union and the United States.
The market was closed on Friday, March 20, for the additional Hari Raya Aidilfitri public holiday.
On a Friday-to-Thursday basis, the April 2026 contract jumped RM41 over the week to RM4,580 per tonne, May 2026 rose RM47 to RM4,619 per tonne, and June 2026 increased RM39 to RM4,611 per tonne.
The July 2026 contract gained RM26 to RM4,580 per tonne, August 2026 added RM19 to RM4,542 per tonne, and September 2026 stood at RM4,504 per tonne.
The weekly trading volume fell to 448,388 lots from 732,935 lots in the week before, while open interest edged up to 237,306 contracts on Thursday from 225,355 on Friday last week.
The physical CPO price for April South increased by RM100 to RM4,600 a tonne.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: On Hari Raya duty, maritime personnel sacrifice family celebrations
Article:
PORT KLANG: While millions of Malaysians celebrate Hari Raya Aidilfitri, maritime personnel remain on duty at sea and on land to safeguard the nation's waters and combat smuggling and illegal fishing.
Selangor Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) officers sacrifice time with family and festive meals as they continue duties throughout the celebrations.
Commanding officer of KM Danga, Lieutenant Commander Muhammad Rasul Abu Bakar, said the vessel has been deployed under ongoing maritime operations since March 17 and will remain on duty through the festive period.
He said the patrol under Ops Pagar Laut will conclude on March 24, when the vessel is relieved by another team.
KM Danga operates with a crew of 23 personnel under Rasul's command.
KM Danga is a MMEA patrol vessel assigned to safeguard national maritime zones, particularly in the busy waters off Selangor and the Straits of Malacca.
[[nid:1401812]]
It conducts continuous sea patrols, enforces maritime laws, and responds to incidents such as smuggling, illegal fishing and search and rescue operations.
KM Danga is key to maintaining maritime security, especially during peak festive periods when enforcement presence is critical.
"This means we will only be able to return to the mainland after that, and only then reunite with our families," he told the New Straits Times.
Rasul said being away during Hari Raya was emotionally challenging for his family.
"They feel sad, of course. But this is part of our responsibility in ensuring the country's security. Throughout my career, I have had to be on duty during festive seasons several times," he said.
The Melaka-born officer, whose wife and seven-year-old child are in Johor Baru, said the separation was particularly difficult for his young son.
"Before I left, my son asked if I would be celebrating Hari Raya with him. I had to tell him I couldn't because I had to work. He hugged me and didn't want me to leave," he said, adding that his son later fell ill after his departure.
Despite the sacrifice, Rasul said such duties were part of service in the maritime force.
Upon completing his assignment, he plans to visit his mother in Melaka before reuniting with his wife and child in Johor.
"Once I return, we will celebrate simply, spending time together, visiting relatives and making up for the time apart," he said.
[[nid:1401811]]
Meanwhile, on land, Bintara Muda Hamdan Makmor Sirat is among those ensuring operations continue at the Selangor MMEA headquarters in Klang.
Serving as duty officer on the second day of Hari Raya, Hamdan said being away from his family during the festive season was not new, having served nearly 20 years in the force.
"This is not the first time I've been away from my family during Raya. It's part of the job," he said.
His wife and two children, aged 13 and eight, are in Melaka, where he plans a brief day trip before returning to duty the following day.
"Honestly, what I miss most is my wife's cooking and being able to sit down and eat together as a family," he said.
Hamdan, who maintains a long-distance relationship with his family, said he is only able to return home two to three times a month.
"They were sad and disappointed that I had to work this Hari Raya, but this is not a burden. It is a duty and responsibility entrusted to us," he said.
He added that eight personnel at the Selangor MMEA headquarters are responsible for maintaining security at the facility, ensuring the welfare of detainees in the lock-up and monitoring seized boats at the jetty.
Additionally, two standby teams comprising 12 personnel are positioned at Pulau Indah for any incidents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Energy supply stable despite global uncertainty
Article:
KUCHING: The Federal Government has given an assurance that the country's energy supply remains stable despite global uncertainties following conflicts in West Asia, said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof.
He said Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) has formulated alternative plans to ensure the continuity of oil and gas supply if key routes, such as the Strait of Hormuz, are disrupted.
"So far, the country's energy supply remains at a stable level due to early planning and diversification of supply sources.
"The government continues to bear large fuel subsidies to ensure that prices remain stable and do not burden the people despite rising global market prices," he told reporters at a reception organised by the village heads and community leaders in the Petra Jaya parliamentary constituency here today.
Fadillah, who is also the Minister of Energy Transition and Water Transformation, said the price difference in fuel between Malaysia and neighbouring countries has led to smuggling activities, causing losses to the country and affecting domestic supplies.
He said close monitoring would continue to be enhanced to ensure the domestic supply is sufficient and to ensure compliance with regulations by all parties involved in the industry.
In another development, Fadillah said the country is facing uncertain weather conditions due to the transition from the Northeast Monsoon to the Southwest Monsoon, resulting in hotter and drier conditions that could lead to drought.
"The government, through relevant ministries, is closely monitoring water supply together with water providers in each state to ensure sufficient supply.
"So far, the water supply situation is still under control, but continuous monitoring measures will be implemented to deal with any eventuality," he said.
He added that if there is a supply shortage, immediate measures, including water delivery using tanker trucks and monitoring of reservoirs, will be implemented to ensure that the supply reaches consumers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Musk launches 'Terafab' project to make own AI chips
Article:
WASHINGTON: Elon Musk announced Saturday a plan to make chips for artificial intelligence, robotics and data centers in space, in the latest bold project by the world's richest person.
The "Terafab", a manufacturing facility based near Austin, Texas, will aim to produce one terawatt of computing power per year, Musk said.
A terawatt is equivalent to one trillion watts. That is slightly less than the total power generation capacity of the United States, according to an industry group.
Musk said the project would be run jointly by his electric-vehicle firm Tesla and his rocket company SpaceX.
He did not disclose the initial investment. Previous US media reports have put the figure between US$20 billion and US$25 billion.
Musk, who has no prior experience in semiconductors, said the Terafab was necessary because Tesla and SpaceX's demand for computing power was expected to far exceed that of global chip suppliers.
"We're very grateful to our existing supply chain, to Samsung, TSMC, Micron, and others... but there's a maximum rate at which they're comfortable expanding," Musk said.
"That rate is much less than we would like... and we need the chips, so we're going to build the Terafab."
An "advanced technology fab" in Austin will have the facilities to design, manufacture, test and improve each chip, Musk said.
Eventually, the project aims to make chips to support 100 to 200 gigawatts of computing power on Earth, and a terawatt in space.
Musk did not give a timeline for the Terafab's output, and has previously promised grand results from other projects on compressed time scales.
He said the Terafab would ultimately help humanity become a "galactic civilization" capable of harnessing the resources of other planets and stars.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geopolitical Fuel for China's Electric Ambitions: A Malaysian Perspective
Article:
THE first time the West decided to stir things up in the Middle East in the 1970s, it led to a crisis in oil supply that saw pumps bled dry and queues forming for blocks.
That, in turn, led to a heightened interest in small, economical cars.
The biggest beneficiaries of the 1973 oil embargo were Japanese carmakers, who had exactly the right product for those who no longer wanted to feed thirsty V8s.
It hurt the US automotive industry so deeply that it never truly recovered.
The "Big Three" were only saved by the administration's clever exclusion of "farm vehicles" from punitive fuel economy calculations.
Consequently, farm trucks evolved into the SUVs and pickups we see today, keeping American factories humming, while the average wage earner steadily transitioned to smaller, more efficient Japanese cars.
Within 20 years, giants like Toyota, Honda and Nissan dominated the streets of the world.
History repeated itself when the Koreans took advantage of erratic oil prices during America's second venture into the Middle East at the start of the millennium.
Every time oil prices reach record levels, fuel efficiency improves - not necessarily to save the planet, but to save our pockets.
Chokehold On The Straits
Now, we face a third, more volatile act. With the Straits of Hormuz effectively choked by Iran as retaliation against Western aggression, oil prices are primed for a sustained surge.
The prospect of a diplomatic off-ramp seems distant.
For Malaysia, the timing is particularly cruel. We are currently in the midst of a delicate "subsidy rationalisation" dance.
As of early 2026, the price of non-subsidised RON95 is already creeping upward, and RON97 is flirting with the RM4.00 mark.
While the government's BUDI95 program still cushions eligible Malaysians, the Prime Minister has already signaled that this fiscal ceiling is only sustainable for as long as the global market allows.
If the "black stuff" stays above US$120 per barrel, the fiscal burden of maintaining subsidised fuel will become a black hole in our national budget.
We aren't just watching a war on the news; we are watching the potential evaporation of the Malaysian lifestyle of "cheap" motoring.
A Math Problem The West Can't Solve
This time, the conflict is costing the world roughly 20 million barrels of oil per day.
Given that daily global consumption sits around 106 million barrels, this is a monster drop in supply. In percentage terms, this contraction is more severe than the 1973 embargo.
As petrol dealers in Petaling Jaya and Johor Bahru begin making arrangements for additional rolling capital to buy fuel at higher costs, the Malaysian motorist is doing the math.
And the math leads to a singular, inevitable conclusion: the internal combustion engine (ICE) is becoming a liability.
This is where the geopolitical irony tastes most bitter. For the last decade, Western legacy automakers - and even some of our traditional favourites from Japan - have hedged their bets.
They lobbied for slower transitions and relied on the high margins of gas-guzzling SUVs. They assumed they had time.
China, however, did not wait. And increasingly, Malaysia is becoming the front line of their global expansion.
The Dragon In The Driveway
While the West was preoccupied with policing the Middle East, China was quietly securing the entire battery supply chain.
They built the infrastructure for a post-oil world while the West was still arguing over the validity of climate change.
If the 1970s belonged to Japan and the 2000s to Korea, the 2020s are being handed to China on a silver platter.
In Malaysia, we are already seeing the shift. In the first half of 2025, the Proton e.Mas 5 - a product of the Geely-Proton partnership - surpassed 5,000 deliveries in two months, becoming the nation's top-selling EV.
When fuel becomes a scarcity, the consumer's primary barrier to EV adoption - range anxiety - is quickly replaced by "price anxiety" at the pump.
In this new reality, a Chinese-engineered EV doesn't just look like a tech-forward gadget; it looks like a life-raft.
By forcing a global oil spike through conflict with Iran, the West is effectively subsidising the marketing campaign for Chinese electric vehicles in markets like ours.
The OMV "Knife Twist"
But there is a final, sharper twist for the Malaysian car buyer.
For years, the Ministry of Finance has "kicked the can down the road" regarding the revised Open Market Value (OMV) calculations.
This new methodology - P.U. (A) 402/2019 - aims to include non-manufacturing costs like marketing and dealer margins into the taxable value of a car.
The industry has long warned that when this tax is fully implemented, the price of locally assembled (CKD) petrol cars could jump by 10-30 per cent. For a mid-sized Japanese SUV, that could mean an overnight price hike of RM20,000 or more.
At least that is what the Malaysian Automotive Association says, whereas the government side assures us that the price hike will be minimal and it will still benefit locally assembled products.
While the government has extended the current "tax holiday" on these costs until June 2026, the cliff-edge is approaching.
At the same time, the 2026 budget has introduced a new, power-based road tax structure for EVs that is significantly cheaper than the old petrol-displacement formula.
For example, a BYD Atto 3 or a Proton e.Mas 7 will see annual road tax as low as RM160 to RM200 - comparable to a basic 1.5L Vios or City, but with the performance of a 2.5L executive sedan.
The pincer movement is now complete. On one side, the West's conflict with Iran is driving the price of petrol toward RM4.00.
On the other, the looming OMV tax correction is about to make petrol-powered cars significantly more expensive to buy.
Conclusion: The End Of The ICE Age
We are witnessing the closing of a circle. The automotive industry was built on the cheap, reliable flow of Middle Eastern oil.
As that flow turns into a trickle and the price becomes a political weapon, the industry's centre of gravity is shifting East - further East than Tokyo or Seoul.
By the time the dust settles in the Middle East, the Western giants may find themselves holding the keys to a kingdom that no longer exists.
For the Malaysian consumer, the choice is no longer about "saving the environment."
It's about survival. Between the OMV price hikes and the pump prices driven by a war 6,000 kilometres away, the petrol engine is being taxed and fueled into extinction.
The West's war with Iran may not just reshape the map of the Middle East; it is permanently rewriting the logos on the grilles in our driveways.
The keys to the future aren't being forged in the fires of conflict, but in the battery labs of the Middle Kingdom.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heatwave grips parts of country as northeast monsoon nears end
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is entering the tail end of the Northeast Monsoon, a transitional phase marked by reduced rainfall and rising temperatures that have triggered heatwaves in several areas.
Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) Institute of Climate Change senior lecturer Dr Ricky Kemarau said the shift is being felt as several locations record unusually high daily temperatures, raising public health concerns.
He said the current conditions align with climate patterns typical of the monsoon's end.
"A Level 2 heatwave status has been recorded in Padang Terap, Kedah, where daily maximum temperatures have ranged between 37°C and 40°C for at least three consecutive days.
"This meets the official definition set by the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) to classify a heatwave," he said when contacted today.
He added that several other areas are currently under Level 1 (alert) status, with daily maximum temperatures hitting between 35°C and 37°C over the same period.
"This indicates that hot conditions are prevalent and could escalate if they persist," he said.
MetMalaysia data shows some areas have experienced dry spells lasting over a week, contributing to a drier atmosphere.
Ricky explained that when rainfall decreases, cloud cover reduces and natural cooling mechanisms such as soil moisture and rain-induced cooling weaken.
"In such conditions, more thermal energy is stored as sensible heat on the earth's surface, causing temperatures to rise continuously," he said.
He warned that the heat poses serious health risks, including heatstroke, dehydration, and heat exhaustion, particularly for senior citizens, children, and outdoor workers.
"The public is advised to take precautions, including limiting outdoor activities between 11am and 4pm and ensuring adequate water intake even if they do not feel thirsty.
"Use protection such as hats or appropriate clothing and stay alert for early signs of heat stress, such as dizziness, extreme fatigue, or confusion," he said.
MetMalaysia previously issued a Level 2 heatwave warning for Padang Terap.
It also identified 12 areas in Peninsular Malaysia under Level 1 (alert) status, involving Perlis and several districts in Kedah, including Baling, Sik, Pendang, Pokok Sena, Kubang Pasu, Langkawi, Kuala Muda, and Bandar Baharu.
Similar alerts were issued for the Northeast district in Penang, Hulu Perak (Perak), and Rembau (Negri Sembilan).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran threatens Middle East infrastructure after Trump ultimatum
Article:
TEHRAN : Iran has threatened to attack key Middle East infrastructure if US President Donald Trump fulfills a vow to "obliterate" the republic's power plants unless the Strait of Hormuz is reopened within 48 hours.
Trump ratcheted up pressure on Tehran on Sunday, announcing a countdown over the de facto blockade of the crucial trade route.
"If Iran doesn't FULLY OPEN, WITHOUT THREAT, the Strait of Hormuz, within 48 HOURS from this exact point in time, the United States of America will hit and obliterate their various POWER PLANTS, STARTING WITH THE BIGGEST ONE FIRST!", Trump posted on his Truth Social platform.
Iran's military operational command responded that if the country's facilities were hit, "all energy, information technology and desalination infrastructure belonging to the US" in the region would be targeted.
Tehran's defiance follows its retaliation for an attack on its Natanz nuclear site with two direct hits on southern Israel. The missiles slipped past air defences and crashed into Dimona and Arad, wounding more than 100 people.
Israel said it launched a fresh wave of strikes on Tehran on Sunday in response. Trump has since turned his attention to the blockaded strait, which typically carries a fifth of the global crude oil trade.
The standoff has sent North Sea Brent crude trading above US$105 a barrel. Trump slammed NATO allies as "cowards" and urged them to secure the waterway.
On Saturday, 22 countries – including the UK, France, Italy, Germany, South Korea, Australia, the UAE and Bahrain – condemned the "de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iranian forces".
As thousands more American Marines head to the Middle East, US Central Command said bunker-busting bombs were dropped on an underground Iranian coastal facility this week, degrading Iran's ability to threaten freedom of navigation.
'This was terrifying'
Iran's missile attack on Israel indicated its arsenal remains a threat, even after Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu claimed to have decimated Tehran's forces.
The hits on Arad and Dimona tore open residential buildings and carved craters into the ground.
AFP footage from Arad showed rescue workers sifting through rubble in a bombed-out building.
"There was a 'boom, boom!', my mother was screaming.
"This was terrifying... this town had never seen anything like this," 17-year-old Arad resident Ido Franky said.
Dimona hosts what is widely believed to be the Middle East's only nuclear arsenal, though Israel has never admitted to having nuclear weapons.
Rescuers said the missile fell about 5km from the facility, wounding about 30 people.
UN nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi reiterated his call for "military restraint to avoid any risk of a nuclear accident."
Meanwhile, Iran has continued retaliatory attacks on Gulf nations it accuses of serving as a launchpad for US strikes.
Saudi Arabia said on Sunday it detected three ballistic missiles near Riyadh and intercepted three drones.
The United Arab Emirates said it responded to new missile and drone attacks after the republic warned its neighbour against allowing strikes from disputed islands.
Iran also launched an unsuccessful ballistic-missile attack on the US-UK base at Diego Garcia, about 4,000km away, a UK official said. Iran has not claimed this attack.
Remarkable endurance?
Analysts said Iran's government has survived the loss of its top leaders and its strike capacity is proving durable.
"They're showing a lot of resilience that we didn't perhaps expect... when it took this on," Neil Quilliam of Chatham House said.
Tehran marked the end of Ramadan and the Persian New Year, Nowruz, as the war entered its fourth week.
While the supreme leader traditionally leads Eid al-Fitr prayers, Mojtaba Khamenei – who came to power this month after his father Ali Khamenei was killed – remained out of the public eye.
Instead, head of the judiciary Gholam Hossein Mohseni Ejei attended prayers at the Imam Khomeini grand mosque.
"The atmosphere of the New Year was spreading through the city," said Farid, an advertising executive.
"But the thought that some people could be dying right at the New Year dinner table was painful."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teen pillion rider killed in Banting motorcycle crash
Article:
BANTING: An 18-year-old pillion rider was killed and the rider injured, in a road crash at Km2, Jalan Olak Lempit-KLIA here.
Kuala Langat police chief Superintendent Mohd Akmalrizal Radzi said the district Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department received information involving a red Honda motorcycle about 1.15pm yesterday.
"Preliminary investigations found that the motorcycle was travelling from Banting towards Olak Lempit when the rider is believed to have lost control of the vehicle.
"The motorcycle then scraped against a road barrier, causing both the rider and pillion rider to be thrown onto the road," he said in a statement.
He said the 18-year-old female pillion rider died at the scene, while the 18-year-old female rider suffered injuries to her hands and legs.
The injured rider was taken to Banting Hospital for treatment.
Akmalrizal said the victim's family had gone to the Kuala Langat TEID to lodge a police report.
"The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987," he said.
Those with information on the incident have been urged to contact investigating officer Inspector Mazrol Mohd Din or go to the nearest police station to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elon Musk offers to pay TSA salaries amid budget battle and airport line‑ups
Article:
WASHINGTON: Billionaire entrepreneur Elon Musk, the world's richest person, said on Saturday he would cover the paychecks of US Transportation Security Administration officers amid their second unpaid work stoppage in six months as a protracted federal funding lapse persists.
The budget impasse over funding for the TSA's parent agency, the Department of Homeland Security, is in its fifth week. Screeners and other TSA personnel are days away from missing a second full paycheck, yet are being pressured to show up as screening times at some airports stretch on for hours.
"I would like to offer to pay the salaries of TSA personnel during this funding impasse that is negatively affecting the lives of so many Americans at airports throughout the country," Musk said in a post on his social media platform X.
Representatives for the Department of Homeland Security, the TSA and Musk did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Airlines and travel groups say absences among the TSA's roughly 50,000 airport security officers could increase again this weekend.
TSA staffers earn an average of US$61,000 annually, according to federal data, and airports are running food drives and accepting donations to support security screeners amid the partial US government shutdown.
DHS funding timelines remain uncertain. Senate Majority Leader John Thune said on Friday that bipartisan negotiators had narrowed remaining disputes over DHS funding, but a deal had not been finalised.
In related controversy, Democrats in Congress earlier this year agreed to fund most of the government in exchange for changes to DHS policy after two US citizens were killed by federal immigration agents during enforcement operations — an incident that sparked widespread debate and protests.
Context on recent Minnesota incidents involving ICE agents (for background)
Multiple federal immigration enforcement actions in Minnesota have drawn national attention in early 2026. On Jan 7, Renée Good, a 37‑year‑old US citizen, was fatally shot by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent during an operation in Minneapolis, triggering protests and scrutiny of federal use of force.
Another shooting in Jan 2026 saw a Venezuelan man, Julio Cesar Sosa‑Celis, wounded by an ICE agent, with videos released that contradicted initial government accounts of the encounter.
These incidents have contributed to ongoing debates about the role and accountability of federal immigration agents and have intensified political tensions over DHS operations amid the broader budget and security impasse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran threatens Middle East infrastructure after Trump ultimatum
Article:
TEHRAN: Iran on Sunday threatened to attack key infrastructure across the Middle East if US President Donald Trump follows through on his vow to "obliterate" the Islamic republic's power plants unless the Strait of Hormuz swiftly reopens.
Trump, after signalling he may wind down the war, ratcheted up pressure on Iran's leadership, announcing a countdown over the Islamic republic's de facto blockade on the crucial trade route.
"If Iran doesn't FULLY OPEN, WITHOUT THREAT, the Strait of Hormuz, within 48 HOURS from this exact point in time, the United States of America will hit and obliterate their various POWER PLANTS, STARTING WITH THE BIGGEST ONE FIRST!" Trump posted on his Truth Social platform.
But Iran's military operational command responded that if the country's facilities were hit, "all energy, information technology and desalination infrastructure belonging to the US" in the region would be targeted.
Tehran's defiance comes after it retaliated for an attack on its nuclear site at Natanz with two direct hits on southern Israel.
Slipping past the country's air defences, the missiles crashed into the towns of Dimona, which hosts a nuclear facility, and Arad, wounding more than 100 people.
Israel said it launched a fresh wave of strikes on the Iranian capital, Tehran, on Sunday in response.
But Trump turned his attention to the blockaded strait, which typically carries a fifth of the global crude oil trade.
The standoff has rattled markets and sent oil prices soaring, with North Sea Brent crude now trading above $105 a barrel, as concerns grow about the long-term consequences for the global economy.
Trump has slammed NATO allies as "cowards" and urged them to secure the strait.
A total of 22 countries – including the UK, France, Italy, Germany, South Korea, Australia, the UAE and Bahrain – condemned on Saturday the "de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iranian forces".
As thousands more American Marines head to the Middle East, US Central Command said bunker-busting bombs were dropped on an underground Iranian coastal facility this week, degrading Iran's ability to threaten freedom of navigation in the strategic waterway.
- 'This was terrifying.'
Iran's missile attack on Israel indicated that its arsenal still poses a threat across the region, even after Trump and Netanyahu claimed to have decimated Tehran's forces.
The Iranian hits on the towns of Arad and Dimona tore open the fronts of residential buildings and carved craters into the ground – among the most destructive attacks of the three-week war.
AFP footage from Arad showed rescue workers sifting through rubble for wounded people in a bombed-out building.
"There was a 'boom, boom!' My mother was screaming," 17-year-old Arad resident Ido Franky told AFP near the impact site, where an AFP correspondent saw three damaged buildings and firefighters reported a blaze.
"This was terrifying... this town had never seen anything like this."
Dimona hosts what is widely believed to be the Middle East's only nuclear arsenal, although Israel has never admitted to possessing nuclear weapons, insisting the site is for research.
The missile fell about five kilometres from the nuclear facility, leaving about 30 people wounded, according to rescuers.
UN nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi reiterated his call for "military restraint to avoid any risk of a nuclear accident".
Meanwhile, Iran has kept up retaliatory attacks on Gulf nations it accuses of serving as a launchpad for US strikes.
Saudi Arabia said on Sunday it detected three ballistic missiles around the capital, Riyadh, and the kingdom's forces intercepted three drones.
The United Arab Emirates said it responded to new missile and drone attacks from Iran after the Islamic Republic warned its neighbour against allowing strikes from disputed islands near the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran also launched an unsuccessful ballistic-missile attack on the US-UK base at Diego Garcia, around 4,000 kilometres (2,500 miles) away, a UK official told AFP – which would have been the longest-range Iranian strike yet had it succeeded.
Iran has not claimed this attack.
- Remarkable endurance? -
Analysts say Iran's government has survived the loss of its top leaders and that its strike capacity is proving more durable than expected.
"They're showing a lot of resilience that we didn't perhaps expect, that the US didn't expect, when it took this on," Neil Quilliam of Chatham House told the think tank's podcast.
Tehran, meanwhile, marked the end of Ramadan and the Persian New Year, Nowruz, as the war entered its fourth week.
Iran's supreme leader traditionally leads Eid al-Fitr prayers, but Mojtaba Khamenei – who came to power earlier this month after his father, Ali Khamenei, was killed – has remained out of the public eye.
Instead, the head of the judiciary, Gholam Hossein Mohseni Ejei, attended prayers at central Tehran's overflowing Imam Khomeini grand mosque.
"The atmosphere of the New Year was spreading through the city," said Farid, an advertising executive reached by AFP through an online message.
But "the thought that some people could be dying right at the New Year dinner table was painful," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China's premier vows to expand global 'trade pie': state media
Article:
BEIJING: China's number two leader Li Qiang said Sunday that his country was willing to help expand the global "trade pie" by further opening up, state media reported, while he slammed unilateralism from certain countries.
Many of China's key trading partners have increasingly called on Beijing to reduce its soaring trade surplus owing to its impact on local competition.
Its trade surged by a fifth in the first two months of the year, official data showed earlier this month, significantly outpacing forecasts.
China "will steadfastly advance high-level opening up, import more high-quality foreign goods, and work alongside all parties to promote the optimised and balanced development of trade", Premier Li Qiang told business executives in Beijing on Sunday, according to Xinhua.
Li was speaking at the opening of the annual China Development Forum, attended this year by prominent business leaders including Apple CEO Tim Cook.
The Chinese premier added that Beijing would work with other countries to "join forces to make the global economic and trade pie larger for everyone."
He slammed growing unilateralism and protectionism, which he said was "no panacea for resolving problems."
Beijing has been seeking to steer a shaky economy onto a more stable path since the end of the pandemic, particularly by boosting consumption.
It had been locked in a blistering trade war last year with Washington after President Donald Trump imposed tariffs on countries including China.
The recent trade boost is a lifeline for China, the world's second-largest economy, as domestic consumer activity has slumped, and adds to the record surplus achieved last year.
The China Development Forum convenes as the Middle East war, triggered by US and Israeli strikes on Iran, rages on.
Tehran has retaliated with strikes across the region and beyond in a conflict that has threatened global energy security as well as China's oil supplies.
Li told the Chinese officials and global business executives the international rules-based order was suffering "severe disruption" with power politics "running rampant."
Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng met with senior representatives of multinational companies including HSBC, UBS, Schneider Electric and Standard Chartered on Saturday, Xinhua reported.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Slovenia votes in tight polls, with conservatives eyeing comeback
Article:
LJUBLJANA: Slovenians began voting on Sunday in tight parliamentary elections, with the conservatives of veteran politician Janez Janša, an admirer of US President Donald Trump, eyeing a comeback.
A Janša return could see the ex-Yugoslav nation, a European Union member of two million people, take an illiberal turn again after four years of centre-left rule under liberal Prime Minister Robert Golob.
Foreign interference claims have shaken the campaign, with authorities probing whether an Israeli intelligence firm was behind secretly recorded videos suggesting alleged graft in Golob's government.
"I think these are the most important elections in Slovenia in a long time," a 26-year-old landscape architect, who only gave her name as Shiva, told AFP this week in the capital, Ljubljana.
The last government of three-time premier Janša – who is also an ally of nationalist Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán – saw mass protests and EU criticism over rule-of-law concerns.
Under Golob, a political newcomer when he took over from Janša in 2022, Slovenia legalised same-sex marriage and became one of the few EU countries to describe Israel's war in Gaza as "genocide."
Polls opened at 7am local time (0600 GMT), according to AFP journalists. They will close at 7pm, with exit polls released just after the closure.
'Slovenian values'
Janša's conservatives long polled ahead of Golob's liberals, but the gap has recently closed, with the two parties now running neck-and-neck according to opinion polls.
In his campaign, Janša, 67, has pledged to restore "Slovenian values" such as the "traditional family" and "close the pipe" of state money to NGOs deemed political parties.
"The Slovenian citizen must be at the forefront, not Palestine, not illegal migrants," Janša said at a public TV debate on Friday, calling the vote "a referendum on corruption."
Gaja Grcar, a 22-year-old student who attended one of Janša's rallies earlier this month, said: "God, homeland and family, those are the Slovenian values."
Golob, 59, said in Friday's debate that voters were deciding "what kind of Slovenia" they want.
"For those who love Slovenia under the free sun, the choice is very clear," said the former power company manager.
[[nid:1401782]]
Election interference claims
Golob on Thursday asked the EU to probe alleged election interference following the publication of a series of secretly recorded videos.
Slovenian authorities are investigating whether Israeli intelligence firm Black Cube was behind the videos, which feature a Slovenian lobbyist, a lawyer, a former minister and a manager.
The videos allegedly show officials suggesting ways to influence decision-makers in Golob's government to speed up procedures or win contracts.
Janša this week admitted to having met a Black Cube official but denied being behind the videos.
On Friday, more than 1,000 people gathered in front of parliament to urge fellow citizens to cast their vote.
"This campaign has been one of the dirtiest and most content-free campaigns in the history of Slovenia… People are disappointed," one of the rally organisers, Jasa Jenull, told AFP.
He warned the country was "facing a slide into totalitarianism."
Far-right parties have made significant gains across Europe in recent years amid economic downturns and fears over Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
"If the right-wing bloc wins, this would mean the fall of another liberal stronghold in Europe, another nail in the liberal democracy's coffin," political commentator Aljaž Pengov Bitenc told AFP.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelantan island folk take to the water for Raya reunions, grave visits
Article:
KOTA BARU: For residents of the islands surrounding Tumpat, the journey home for Hari Raya Aidilfitri begins not on the road, but on the water.
During the festive season, those living in Pulau Beluru, Pulau Teluk Rejuna, and other nearby islets rely on boat services to cross the Kelantan River to reach the mainland.
Many head toward Kota Baru, Pasir Mas, and other districts to celebrate the festivities with loved ones and pay respects at family graves.
At the Kuala Besar jetty near Pantai Cahaya Bulan, more than 10 boats operate daily, ferrying a steady stream of passengers as the "balik kampung" rush gathers pace.
For 38-year-old Mohd Ashraf Rahmat, the annual journey is one he looks forward to despite the distance.
The Raub, Pahang native returns just once a year to Pantai Suri, where his grandmother lives.
"I come back with my wife, our three children and my mother-in-law. It's the only time we can all gather as a family," said the businessman, adding that they would return to Raub after the celebrations.
For others, the boat ride is part of a routine deeply tied to family bonds.
Zainal Ahmad Yusoff, 52, travels from Pulau Beluru to Pasir Mas to visit relatives, including his mother-in-law, sister-in-law and nephews.
"Today, 14 of us are travelling together. Some of my family members are coming from Perlis. We make this trip at least twice a year," said the trader.
Self-employed Mohd Fadli Saudi from Pulau Teluk Rejuna said his journey during Ramadan carries a more solemn purpose.
He makes the crossing to visit the grave of his late grandmother at the Che Latiff cemetery near Kuala Besar.
"I will go every Ramadan. It's important for me to remember and pray for her," he said.
Despite modern development and improved connectivity in many parts of the country, these island communities continue to depend on boats — a tradition that remains central to their way of life during festive seasons.
For them, the journey across the water is more than a commute; it is a passage that reconnects them with family, memories, and heritage.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 12 killed, dozens injured as bus collides with train in southeastern Bangladesh
Article:
DHAKA: At least 12 people were killed and 26 others injured in a bus-train collision in the southeastern Comilla district early Sunday, fire service officials and police said,   Anadolu Ajansi reported.
Those dead included two women and three children, Fire Service and Civil Defence Deputy Assistant Director (Comilla) Mohammad Idris told Anadolu, and 10 of the injured remain in hospitals.
A Dhaka-bound one-up train leaving port city Chittagong collided with a bus heading towards Chittagong from western Chapainawabganj district at the Paduar Bazar rail crossing area in Comilla city.
The train dragged the bus one kilometre away.
Idris said: "We believe that the accident occurred when the bus went onto the railway line due to the lack of a signal at the Padua Bazar rail crossing."
The Bangladesh Railway formed two investigation committees to probe the accident and suspended two gatemen of the rail crossings, according to local media reports.
Bangladesh usually sees a huge rush through train stations, buses, and launch terminals, particularly in Dhaka, as people leave for villages to spend Eid vacation with families and relatives, putting a strain on the fragile transportation systems.
On March 18, about 66 people were injured when the Nilsagar Express, a major northern Bengal route train, derailed in the Bogra district's Bagbaria area. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ramanan hails four Malaysian students for Bangkok innovation win
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri Ramanan Ramakrishnan has praised four Malaysian students for their success at the Bangkok Innovation Awards, where the team secured two gold medals and second place in the Primary School Engineering category.
He said the achievement highlights the creative and high-impact ideas of the younger generation on the international stage.
The four students are Ilaneyan Yuvarajan and Theerchanna Muniandy from SJKT KO Sarangapany, alongside Ilanarun Yuvarajan and Elamaaran Muniandy from SMK Sultan Badlishah.
"This achievement is not only a source of pride but also proof that our youth possess creative, high-impact ideas capable of gaining international recognition," he said in a statement.
[[nid:1401768]]
He described the win as a symbol of the younger generation's ability to elevate the nation's status through innovation and academic excellence.
Ramanan, who is also Sungai Buloh member of Parliament, also paid tribute to the students' parents, who travelled to Bangkok to provide moral support.
"The sacrifice and encouragement of families are the backbone of these children's success. It proves that success is not merely an individual effort, but the result of solid collective support," he added.
The PKR vice-president also extended his gratitude to the teachers who guided the students throughout their journey to the international platform.
Ramanan shared a touching moment during the meeting when the students asked for his autograph as a memento.
"While unexpected, I accepted it as a great honour. I took the opportunity to offer words of encouragement so they continue to excel in their bright future," he said.
He expressed hope that this success would serve as a catalyst for more Malaysian youths to innovate and compete globally, further strengthening the country's competitive standing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Probe launched after video of police being provoked goes viral
Article:
KUALA KRAI: Police are investigating alleged provocation against personnel involved in a Hari Raya operation on Friday night after videos of the incident went viral.
Kuala Krai police chief Superintendent Mazlan Mamat said the footage shows members of the public provoking policemen during the 10pm operation aimed at ensuring public order.
"Around midnight, police instructed the public to disperse and stop using banned firecrackers. However, a group remained defiant and continued gathering along the road," he said.
He said that at 2am, police detected motorcyclists and drivers racing between the Batu Balai and Guchil traffic lights.
"During an inspection at Dataran Guchil, a motorcyclist fell after colliding with another motorcycle while attempting to evade police checks."
A policeman from the Kuala Krai police station attempted to detain the motorcyclist, but a struggle ensued, injuring the policeman, he said in a statement.
Mazlan said a group later gathered at the scene and interfered with police duties, with some allegedly provoking and threatening the officer.
"Police managed the situation professionally and exercised restraint to prevent any untoward incidents. Control measures were taken to ensure the situation did not escalate and public safety remained intact," he said.
The operation concluded at 6am, with three cars and three motorcycles seized and 34 summonses issued. Two people were arrested.
Mazlan said police are tracking several vehicles and individuals believed to have been involved who managed to flee.
An investigation has been opened under Section 186 and Section 353 of the Penal Code for obstructing a public servant in discharging their duty and using criminal force to deter a public servant.
The offences are punishable by jail terms, fines or both.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Immigration Dept begins 24-hour monitoring of KL 'hotspots' for Hari Raya
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Immigration Department has commenced 24-hour monitoring of foreigner "hotspots" in the federal capital to ensure public safety and order during the Aidilfitri festive season.
The surveillance, conducted under Ops Pemantauan from March 20 to 29, focuses on high-traffic areas and tourist attractions.
Monitored locations include KLCC, TRX, SOGO, Pavilion, Bukit Bintang, Central Market, Petaling Street, Kotaraya, Masjid India, Chow Kit, and Kampung Baru.
Patrols are also being carried out along major roads using department vehicles equipped with beacon lights to maintain a visible enforcement presence.
Affected routes include Jalan Ampang, Jalan Sultan Ismail, Jalan Imbi, Jalan Kia Peng, Jalan Changkat, Jalan Alor, Jalan Pudu, Jalan Silang, Jalan Tun HS Lee, Jalan Raja Laut, and Jalan Masjid India.
Immigration director-general Datuk Zakaria Shaaban said the monitoring is not intended to hinder or disrupt foreigners from celebrating the festivities.
"It is a proactive measure to ensure they comply with immigration rules and laws. The department's responsibility is to monitor tourists and foreign nationals to ensure public safety and order are maintained," he said in a statement today.
The operation involves 50 immigration officers of various ranks working 12-hour shifts, with the first shift running from 10am to 10pm and the second from 10pm to 10am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Contractor loses savings, company funds to fake investment scheme
Article:
KUANTAN: A contractor lost RM356,342 in personal savings and company funds after falling victim to a fraudulent investment scheme.
Pahang police chief Datuk Seri Yahaya Othman said the 34-year-old victim encountered an investment advertisement on a website known as Marinasand on Feb 18.
He said the victim registered for the scheme, which promised 10 per cent returns on investments.
Between Feb 18 and March 19, the victim carried out 16 transactions to five different bank accounts involving RM356,342.
The victim received an initial return of RM1,000 before the promised profits stopped.
He later realised he had been cheated after he could no longer access the investment website.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating, which carries a jail term of between one and 10 years, whipping, and a fine upon conviction.
Yahaya urged the public to verify bank account numbers through the https://semakmule.rmp.gov.my/ website before performing any financial transactions.
"Those who suspect they have been scammed should immediately contact the National Scam Response Centre at 997 for further action," he said in a statement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Samsuri calls for non-partisan council to tackle global economic impact
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Perikatan Nasional chairman Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar has called for a non-partisan approach to shield the country from the escalating global economic uncertainty caused by conflicts in the Middle East.
He proposed the establishment of a special platform, such as a National Economic Consultation and Intervention Council, to involve all stakeholders in addressing these challenges.
Samsuri said the ongoing regional conflicts have begun to directly impact the world economy, including Malaysia, by driving up energy prices, disrupting supply chains, and straining global markets.
He noted that such volatility has spiked logistics costs, subsequently increasing production and operational expenses across various economic sectors.
"Malaysia needs a non-partisan approach that prioritises national interests over partisan politics, ensuring it is inclusive and action-oriented.
"I propose the comprehensive involvement of all parties, including the government, the opposition, non-governmental organisations, academics, industry players, and civil society representatives in a forum like the National Economic Consultation and Intervention Council," he said in a statement today.
Samsuri, who is also Terengganu Menteri Besar, said such a move is vital to ensure national decisions reflect collective interests.
"This is not about politics; it is about national survival. In an increasingly uncertain world, Malaysia cannot afford to be divided along party lines.
"Instead, we must gather the nation's collective strength, transcending political differences, to face these challenges together. When national economic stability and the people's welfare are at stake, our priority must be Malaysia's resilience," he said.
The Kemaman member of Parliament warned that the ripple effects of global instability are unavoidable.
He said the country could face hikes in the prices of basic goods and food, alongside rising transport and utility costs, in the coming months.
"The greater impact includes risks to the employment sector, with the possibility of workforce reductions in affected industries," he said.
He added that without proactive policy intervention, the risk of retrenchments is expected to rise, particularly in the manufacturing, logistics, and small and medium enterprise sectors, which are most vulnerable to surging raw material costs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man dies after falling into drain in Klang
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A man in his 40s died after falling into a drain at Jalan Kasban in Taman Meru Makmur, Klang, today.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department operations centre chief Cik Hamdan Hamzah said the department received a distress call at 7.45am and dispatched a team to the scene.
Firemen found the victim in the drain upon arrival.
The victim was later pronounced dead, Cik Hamdan said when contacted.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two more Malaysians arrested in Singapore over impersonation scams
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Two Malaysian men were arrested in Singapore yesterday for their suspected involvement in scams involving the
impersonation of government officials.
According to a report by The Straits Times, this brings the total number of Malaysians arrested in connection with such cases this month to eight.
A 30-year-old suspect is expected to be charged today with assisting another to retain benefits from criminal conduct. The second suspect, aged 20, is slated to be charged with the same offence on March 23.
If convicted, they face up to 10 years in jail, a fine of up to SGD500,000 (RM1.54 million), or both.
Singapore police said the arrests follow a surge in Malaysians travelling to the republic to assist scam syndicates in collecting cash and valuables from victims.
The duo was arrested for allegedly cheating a 62-year-old woman of 8.6kg of gold through a transnational scam syndicate.
The victim was duped into surrendering the gold at the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) building after scammers, posing as MAS and telecommunications officials, had claimed she was involved in a money laundering case.
The alleged scammers instructed her to go to the MAS Building in Shenton Way, where the purported officer would identify himself with a codeword and take the gold for inspection.
Suspicious, the victim asked a security guard about the man's identity. Upon being told the man was not affiliated with MAS, she alerted the police.
The 30-year-old suspect was intercepted while attempting to flee the country, while his accomplice was nabbed near Woodlands.
Investigations revealed the duo were purportedly tasked by the syndicate to collect valuables, including cash and jewellery, from victims before handing them over to other unknown individuals.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China's Premier Li pledges national treatment for foreign companies
Article:
BEIJING: Chinese Premier Li Qiang pledged on Sunday to improve the business environment and fully implement national treatment for foreign enterprises.
Here are some details from his speech at the China Development Forum, according to state media:
• Protectionism is by no means a panacea for solving problems, Li said, calling instead for opening up and technological progress to create new markets.
• Li said China's competitive advantages stem from deepening reform and innovation, not subsidies and protection, crediting the hard work of Chinese people and businesses.
• He said China will import more high-quality foreign goods and work with all parties to promote balanced trade development and expand the global trade pie.
• Li described China as committed to being a "cornerstone of certainty" and "harbour of stability" for the world.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Brothers nabbed stealing underground cables in Sungai Petani
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: Two brothers were arrested red-handed while attempting to steal underground cables near the clock tower at Jalan Tunku Ibrahim yesterday afternoon.
The siblings, aged 42 and 44, were caught by a patrolling team from the Kuala Muda district police headquarters Criminal Investigation Department at 4.30pm on the second day of Hari Raya Aidilfitri.
Kuala Muda police chief Assistant Commissioner Hanyan Ramlan said officers spotted the men opening a manhole cover and climbing underground to cut the cables.
"Police managed to apprehend both suspects at the scene. We also seized several tools believed to have been used in the attempted theft," he said in a statement today.
Checks revealed that both suspects possess 12 prior drug offences each.
The brothers has been taken to the Kuala Muda district police headquarters for further investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sri Lanka raises fuel prices by 25pct as war bites
Article:
COLOMBO: Sri Lanka raised fuel prices by 25 per cent on Sunday, the second increase in two weeks, as the country prepared for more impact from the war in the Middle East.
Regular petrol was increased to 398 rupees (US$1.30) per litre, up from 317 rupees, while diesel, the fuel commonly used for public transport, rose by 79 rupees to 382.
Last week, the government ordered an eight percent increase in retail fuel prices and introduced rationing to limit consumption.
"We hope to achieve a 15 to 20 per cent reduction in fuel consumption with the latest increase," an official at the Ceylon Petroleum Corporation said.
He said President Anura Kumara Dissanayake told them last week that the country must prepare for a prolonged conflict in the Middle East that could affect the island's energy supplies.
The president ordered a four-day working week from last Wednesday and asked employers to reintroduce work-from-home arrangements where possible.
The Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway through which some 20 pe rcent of global oil exports pass in peacetime, has been effectively closed by Iran in retaliation over the US and Israeli war against it, now entering its fourth week.
Sri Lanka imports all of its oil and also buys coal for electricity generation.
Sri Lanka buys refined petroleum products from Singapore, Malaysia and South Korea, while crude oil for its Iran-built refinery is sourced from the Middle East.
The government has warned that the fighting in the Middle East, and a prolonged war, could seriously undermine its efforts to emerge from the economic meltdown of 2022.
Sri Lanka defaulted on its US$46 billion foreign debt in 2022 after the country ran out of foreign exchange. Since then, Colombo has secured a US$2.9 billion IMF bailout.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Malaysia still hit by global price surge despite oil production
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim says Malaysia is affected by global oil price fluctuations despite being a producer, as it imports more oil than it exports.
Anwar said questions arose following the rise in global oil prices due to the conflict in West Asia, triggered by Israel and the United States' attacks on Iran.
In a Facebook post, he said the Strait of Hormuz, a key global oil route, has been impacted by the conflict.
"When this route is disrupted, global oil supply becomes limited, and prices surge. Malaysia is also affected, as nearly 50 per cent of the country's oil supply passes through the Strait of Hormuz.
"Although Malaysia is an oil producer, we actually import more oil than we export," he said.
Anwar said to protect the people, government subsidies increased from around RM700 million to RM3.2 billion in less than a week.
He added that the public and most businesses do not have to pay market prices as these subsidies are channelled through Budi Madani Ron95 and Budi Diesel.
"In an increasingly uncertain global situation, efforts to safeguard the welfare and well-being of the people remain a priority for the Madani government," he said.
Previously, Anwar said the subsidised price of RON95 petrol in Malaysia will
remain
at RM1.99 per litre, despite rising global oil prices triggered by the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Coconut water sales soar as heatwave hits Padang Terap
Article:
KUALA NERANG: The scorching heat in Padang Terap has led to a surge in demand for coconut water, with traders reporting rapid sales as temperatures hit 40°C.
Coconut water trader Rosli Musa, 40, said he sold more than 10 coconuts in less than an hour today at his stall near the Bukit Tembaga junction.
"I only started business on the second day of Hari Raya Aidilfitri and didn't expect such an overwhelming response. Customers began stopping by as soon as I opened at 11am to quench their thirst," he said when met today.
Rosli noted that the intense heat, which reportedly reached 40°C yesterday, has made outdoor conditions feel "like skin is burning."
"The peak of the heat is usually between 2pm and 3pm, forcing us to use fans and air conditioning just to stay comfortable," he added.
[[nid:1401718]]
Resident Jamilah Ahmad, 56, observed that the extreme weather has altered traditional festive visits.
"In previous years, I hosted guests under tents outside because of the breeze, but yesterday everyone had to move indoors to escape the heat. Fewer children are also out visiting for duit raya compared to before," she said.
Jamilah added that her household has had to double its ice supply to keep up with the demand for cold drinks.
The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) today maintained a Level 2 heatwave warning for Padang Terap, while 12 other areas in the Peninsula remain at Level 1 (alert).
The 12 areas at Level 1 include Perlis; the Kedah districts of Baling, Sik, Pendang, Pokok Sena, Kubang Pasu, Langkawi, Kuala Muda and Bandar Baharu; the Timur Laut district in Penang; Hulu Perak in Perak; and Rembau in Negri Sembilan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kenanga starts Southern Cable at "Outperform", sees grid upgrade upside
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: KENANGA Research has initiated coverage on Southern Cable Group Bhd, giving it an "Outperform" rating and a target price of RM2.71, based on 18 times financial year 2027 (FY27) earnings per share.
The firm said it views Southern Cable as a key proxy to Malaysia's accelerating grid upgrade cycle.
"With three gigawatts of alternating current energy supply agreements (ESAs) signed in the pipeline, demand for power cables, which are a major component of power systems, should remain strong in the medium term," it said.
Kenanga Research said it projects Southern Cable's earnings to reach RM161 million in FY26 and RM196 million in FY27, reflecting year-on-year growth of 15 per cent and 22 per cent, respectively.
For FY26, the firm expects earnings to be driven mainly by its existing long-term order book of RM500 million, including recent variation orders from Tenaga Nasional Bhd.
It also expects additional support from new purchase orders totalling RM1 billion, underpinned by transmission grid upgrades, utility-scale solar projects, data centre infrastructure and export market expansion.
"We have factored in the group's capacity expansion of 4,000km per year, which will mainly cater to low voltage and medium voltage (MV) cables.
"Contribution from 132 kilovolt (kV) 1,800 square millimeter cables will gradually commence from the second half of FY26, though FY26 contribution from this segment is expected to be modest as deliveries begin to ramp up," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: National Anti‑Drugs Agency reports 81pc recovery rate among 26,852 clients nationwide
Article:
KOTA BHARU, March 22 — A total of 21,794 clients who underwent community-based treatment and rehabilitation programmes, nationwide, achieved permanent recovery status last year.
National Anti-Drugs Agency (AADK) director-general Datuk Ruslin Jusoh said that the recovery rate reached 81 per cent, out of the total 26,852 clients evaluated.
“This achievement proves that AADK’s treatment modules are on the right track, and have exceeded the recovery rate reported by the National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA), which is between 40 and 60 per cent,” he said, when contacted by Bernama recently.
Elaborating further, Ruslin said that last year, a total of 1,500 drug addicts voluntarily reported to the Narcotics Addiction Rehabilitation Centres (Puspen) to undergo treatment and rehabilitation programmes.
“This voluntary admission shows an increasing level of awareness among addicts to seek treatment without coercion.
“It can also be seen following the amendment to the Drug Addicts (Treatment and Rehabilitation) Act 1983, whereby those who come forward voluntarily are not recorded as offenders, but are instead regarded as patients who require treatment,” he said.
He added that, in Kelantan, a total of 147 clients are currently undergoing voluntary treatment and rehabilitation at Puspen Kota Bharu and Bachok. — Bernama

Title: Human Resources minister hails young Malaysians’ international success at Bangkok Innovation Awards
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 22 — Four Malaysian students excelled at the Bangkok Innovation Awards, winning two gold medals and second place in the Engineering category for primary schools.
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan expressed pride over the achievement, describing it as a symbol of the younger generation’s ability to uphold the nation’s dignity through innovation and academic excellence.
“This achievement is not only something to be proud of, but also proves that our young generation possesses creative and high-impact ideas that are capable of being recognised at the international level,” he said in a statement today.
The four students are Ilaneyan Yuvarajan and M. Theerchanna from Sekolah Jenis Kebangsaan Tamil (SJKT) KO Sarangapany, as well as Ilanarun Yuvarajan and M. Elamaaran from Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Sultan Badlishah.
Ramanan, who is also the Member of Parliament for Sungai Buloh, expressed appreciation to the students’ parents who were willing to sacrifice their time to travel to Bangkok to give moral support.
He said the sacrifices and encouragement of family members are the backbone of the students’ success, proving that achievement is not the result of individual effort alone but of strong collective support.
At the same time, the PKR vice-president also conveyed his appreciation to the teachers who guided the students to success on the international stage, and also shared a touching moment when the students asked for his autograph as a keepsake.
“Although unexpected, I accepted it as a great honour. I also took the opportunity to leave them with words of encouragement so that they will continue to go far in their bright future,” he said.
Ramanan expressed hope that the achievement would serve as a catalyst for more Malaysian youths to dare to innovate and achieve success at the global level, further strengthening the country’s position as a competitive nation. — Bernama

Title: Pahang police: Contractor transferred funds 16 times before realising ‘marinasand’ investment was a scam
Article:
TEMERLOH, March 22 — A contractor lost RM356,342 after being believed to have been deceived by a non-existent investment scheme advertised on a website since last month.
Pahang police chief Datuk Seri Yahaya Othman said the victim, a 34-year-old man, saw an investment advertisement on Google on February 18 through a website known as “marinasand”.
“The victim then registered and participated in the investment, which promised returns of 10 per cent on the total investment,” he said in a statement today.
He said the victim had made 16 money transfer transactions using personal savings as well as company funds into five different bank accounts from February 18 to March 19, 2026.
“The victim only received an initial return of around RM1,000 before not receiving any further profits as promised. The victim also found he no longer had access to the investment website before realising he had been scammed,” he said.
In this regard, Yahaya advised the public to consult and seek early advice from the nearest police station before making any investments to avoid becoming victims of fraud.
He also advised the public to check account numbers through the Semak Mule website of the Royal Malaysia Police before making any financial transactions and to immediately contact the National Scam Response Centre at 997 for prompt action if they suspect they have become victims of fraud. — Bernama

Title: Malaysia’s energy supply remains stable despite West Asia tensions, says Fadillah
Article:
KUCHING, March 22 — The Federal Government has given an assurance that the country’s energy supply remains stable despite global uncertainties following conflicts in West Asia, said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof.
He said Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) has formulated alternative plans to ensure the continuity of oil and gas supply if key routes, such as the Strait of Hormuz, are disrupted.
“So far, the country’s energy supply remains at a stable level due to early planning and diversification of supply sources.
“The government continues to bear large fuel subsidies to ensure that prices remain stable and do not burden the people despite rising global market prices,” he told reporters at a reception organised by the village heads and community leaders in the Petra Jaya parliamentary constituency here today.
Fadillah, who is also the Minister of Energy Transition and Water Transformation, said the price difference in fuel between Malaysia and neighbouring countries has led to smuggling activities, causing losses to the country and affecting domestic supplies.
He said close monitoring would continue to be enhanced to ensure the domestic supply is sufficient and to ensure compliance with regulations by all parties involved in the industry.
In another development, Fadillah said the country is facing uncertain weather conditions due to the transition from the Northeast Monsoon to the Southwest Monsoon, resulting in hotter and drier conditions that could lead to drought.
“The government, through relevant ministries, is closely monitoring water supply together with water providers in each state to ensure sufficient supply.
“So far, the water supply situation is still under control, but continuous monitoring measures will be implemented to deal with any eventuality,” he said.
He added that if there is a supply shortage, immediate measures, including water delivery using tanker trucks and monitoring of reservoirs, will be implemented to ensure that the supply reaches consumers. — Bernama

Title: Sarawak studies tougher rabies control as cat infections pose growing challenge
Article:
KUCHING, March 22 — The Sarawak state government is studying the best approaches to strengthen efforts in controlling rabies, particularly infections among cats, said Sarawak Deputy Premier Datuk Amar Dr Sim Kui Hian.
He said discussions have been held with the Sarawak State Health Department and the Department of Veterinary Services Sarawak to develop an integrated action plan.
“Rabies continues to be a serious threat in Sarawak, with about half of the reported bites and scratches involving cats, in addition to stray dogs.
“The main challenge in rabies control now is vaccinating cats, which is more difficult because these animals tend to scratch and their saliva can carry the dangerous virus,” he told reporters at the Majlis Ramah Mesra ceremony organised by the Community Leaders and Heads of Community of the Petra Jaya Parliamentary Constituency here today.
Dr Sim, who is also Sarawak’s Minister for Public Health, Housing and Local Government, reminded the public not to take the rabies threat lightly and to always take preventive measures, including ensuring their pets receive regular vaccinations.
“Immediate action must be taken if bitten by a dog or scratched by a cat, which includes washing the wound with water and seeking treatment at a clinic for assessment and vaccination.
“Rabies vaccines for humans are now available in clinics, allowing treatment to be given quickly and effectively,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Kelantan MB backs cost-saving Raya open houses, says use personal funds over public money
Article:
PASIR MAS, March 22 — Kelantan Menteri Besar Datuk Mohd Nassuruddin Daud expressed support for a cost-saving approach in organising open house events, including using personal funds without relying on government allocations.
He said the approach of using one’s own expenses is in line with the government’s call for celebrations to be held in moderation.
“For the state’s economy, open houses can sometimes be held, so we organise this using personal funds, while government events depend on the situation,” he said when met by reporters at his open house in Meranti here today, which was attended by about 5,000 guests.
Elaborating further, Mohd Nassuruddin said the event was an annual tradition, apart from serving as a platform to strengthen ties between leaders and the people.
On March 11, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim announced that all ministries, agencies and government-linked companies (GLCs) would not be organising open house celebrations in conjunction with Aidilfitri this year, as a cost-saving measure following the global economic uncertainty caused by the conflict in West Asia. — Bernama

Title: Penang CM submits report for Anthony Loke on state quit rent hike, vows media silence
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, March 22 — Penang Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow will prepare a report to be submitted to DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke regarding the implementation of new quit rent (land tax) rates, which came into effect on Jan 1 in the state.
Chow said he will also comply with Loke’s directive not to discuss the issue in the media to avoid negative perceptions and impacts on the party leaderships and the state government as a whole.
“I want to express my appreciation to the DAP secretary-general for arranging a meeting involving all three parties (Chow, Loke and former Penang CM Lim Guan Eng) to discuss the issue of the increase in quit rent rates in Penang, he said in a statement today.
Chow also urged the affected landowners to continue to submit appeals to district and land offices in the state, as well as the office of the director of Lands and Mines at Komtar.
Yesterday, Loke said he would convene a meeting between Chow and Lim to resolve the ongoing dispute over the new quit rent rates in the state, and described their public spat as unhealthy and inappropriate, particularly amid current geopolitical and economic uncertainties.
Following that, Lim, who is also the Bagan Member of Parliament, posted on Facebook that he would temporarily stop making public statements regarding the issue until the meeting involving all three parties is held.
The state government has implemented a review of quit rent rates, and the gazettement of rural to urban land across the state, in accordance with the provisions of Section 101 of the National Land Code (Act 828) effective Jan 1.
The revision of quit rent rates involves nearly 370,000 land titles throughout Penang, covering various land categories and uses, including residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural and other special categories.
Chow had earlier expressed regret that certain parties had sensationalised the increase, without fully understanding the calculation and the actual land use involved. — Bernama
